Minimum floor areas for private amenity space in apartments: Studio 4 sq m; One bedroom 5 sq m; Two bedroom 7 sq m; Three bedroom 9 sq m. (DM HS 7)
open_space.play_space: 100+ dwellings: Areas for younger children (Play area/ground) (min.100m2) OR Multi Use Games Area (tennis/basketball); Landscaped passive recreational area (sitting out); Active amenity open space (Ball Games); Areas for younger children (Play area/ground) (min.200m2); Multi Use Games Area (tennis/basketball); Grass sports pitch/playing fields. (DM HS 5)
community.childcare_provision: Require the provision of childcare facilities of an appropriate type and scale in suitable locations throughout the County and comply with the Section 28 Guidelines on Childcare Facilities, 2001 (and any subsequent update). In particular, the development of childcare facilities at the following locations will normally be required: areas of concentrated employment and business parks; neighbourhood centres; integrated into large retail developments and retail warehouse parks; in, or in the vicinity of, schools or major educational facilities; adjacent to public transport nodes; in, or adjacent to, community centres and within new and existing residential development. (DM CC 1)
community.part_v_pct: Ensure 10% of all lands zoned for residential uses, or for a mixture of residential and other uses, shall be reserved for the provision of social and/or affordable housing in order to address the requirement for social housing under Part V (HPO 33). The percentage reservation for Part V shall be decided on a case-by-case basis of individual site assessment.
community.education: Assessing planning applications for new education facilities and/or redevelopment/extensions of existing schools, the Planning Authority will have regard to ... Site location, proximity of school to catchment area, size of site ... Mobility Management Plan shall be submitted ... Provision of safe and adequate set down etc facilities for buses; Provision of adequate, secure, covered cycle facilities; Provision of safe access and adequate car parking and set down areas to facilitate drop off/pick up ... (DM ED 1). Temporary classrooms will be assessed on a case-by-case basis and will generally be accepted for a temporary period not exceeding five years (DM ED 3). All sites for schools shall comply with 'The Provision of Schools and the Planning System A Code of Practice for Planning Authorities', 2008 (EDPO 1).
community.health: Consider change of use applications from residential to health care facilities / surgeries only where the privacy and amenity of adjacent occupiers can be preserved and the proposal does not have a detrimental effect on local amenity by way of an increase in traffic, car parking or noise. Conversion of part of a dwelling to a medical or dental surgery will normally be permitted where the dwelling remains as the main residence of the practitioner and where a need has been demonstrated. Conversion of the ground floor of a dwelling to a surgery with a separate apartment on the upper floor may be permitted where there are no other more suitable premises available. (DM HC 1)
transport.car_parking_standards: Table 10.3: Land Use Parking Space Requirements — Houses 2 per Dwelling; Apartments/Flats 1.26 - 2 per Unit; Retail 1 per 26m2 gross floor space; Offices 1 per 20m2; Financial Institutions 1 per 20 m2; Retail warehousing 1 per 36 m2; Cash and Carry 2 per 90 m2; Industry 1 per 60 m2 or 1 per 4 employees (greater); Warehousing 1 per 100 m2 or 1 per 4 employees (greater); Hotels/B&B/Guesthouses 1 per bedroom; Bars and Lounges 1 per 7 m2 within speed limit / 2 per 7 m2 outside; Restaurants 1 per table; Take away 6 per unit; Service Station 1 per 300 m2 + shop; Hospitals 1 per bed; Nursing homes 1 per 2 bedrooms; Surgeries/Clinics 2 per consulting room; Childcare/creche/montessori 1 per employee + 0.26 per child; Schools 2 per classroom plus bus circulation. Car parking dimensions: Car parking bay 5m x 2.5m; Loading bay 6m x 3m; Circulation aisles 6m wide (DM TRANS 8).
transport.cycle_parking: Minimum cycle parking (DM TRANS 10): Apartment developments 2 private secure spaces per 100 sq. m; Offices 1 space for every 100 sq. m; Schools 10% of pupil registration numbers, minimum 10 places; Shops 10% of employee numbers (subject to a minimum of 10 bicycle places or one bike space for every car space, whichever is the greater); Off-street parking (incl. Multi-storey) 10% of total car spaces, subject to a minimum of 50 spaces; Public transport pick-up points 5% of daily boarders subject to minimum of 10; Events 5% of forecast attendees. (Also 1 visitor bicycle space per two housing units.)
transport.ev_charging_pct: All developments should provide facilities for the charging of battery-operated cars at a rate of up to 10% of the total car parking spaces. The remainder of the parking spaces should be constructed so as to be capable of accommodating future charging points, as required. New residential development should accommodate at least one car parking space equipped with an EV charging points for every ten car parking spaces being provided for the associated development. (TRANS 34/35)
transport.building_line: Backland infill: Maintains the existing building line and established lengths of site frontage with adjacent dwellings (DM RH 2). Corner/Side garden sites: The maintenance of the front and side building lines, where appropriate (DM HS 18).
transport.stopping_distances_sightlines: New dwellings in the countryside require: a) 30 metres of road frontage, unless a considerable set-back from the roadway exists; c) Sightlines at the proposed entrance must comply with Laois County Council Parking and Roads Standards indicated in Chapter 10 of the Plan and must be achieved within the boundary of the site only. Excessive removal of hedgerow to achieve adequate sightlines is unacceptable. (DM RH 1) Petrol filling stations: Access to filling stations will not be permitted closer than 35 metres to a road junction; Frontage on primary and secondary routes must be at least 20 metres in length; All pumps and installations shall be set back at least 5 metres from the roads (DM TC 3). [No numeric X/Y sightline distances are stated in the chapter — they are deferred to the Roads and Parking Standards.]
transport.universal_access: Disabled Parking (DM TRANS 6): Buildings not normally visited by the public — Minimum of one space in every 26 standard spaces, up to the first 100 spaces, thereafter one space per 100 standard spaces or part thereof. Shops and buildings with public access — Minimum one space in the first 26 standard spaces, minimum 3 in 26-60, five in 60-100 and additional three per every 100 standard spaces in excess. Spaces shall be a minimum of 3.75m wide and 5m long ... The Council will ensure that development of all types provide mobility and access for people with disabilities ... having regard for the National Disability Authority's document 'Building for Everyone: A Universal Design Approach' (2012).
transport.loading_coach_taxi: Ensure adequate space shall be made for the servicing of the facility, including loading and unloading of vehicles, which should be provided for within the site curtilage and should not interfere with the operation of adjacent public Thoroughfares (TRANS 30/31). Loading bay 6m x 3m (DM TRANS 8). Taxi/hackney bases will not be permitted where they are likely to interfere with traffic flows or on street parking ... satisfactory off-street parking facilities are provided when the vehicles are not in use ... Taxi and hackney bases shall have universal access for all. (DM TC 4)
water_environment.suds_requirement: Detailed proposals for Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) should be submitted which demonstrate that the development would not result in surface water discharges affecting neighbouring lands without agreement and/or the public road. The following should be taken into consideration: 1) Store rainwater for later use (rainwater harvesting); 2) Use infiltration techniques such as porous hard surfaces, soft landscaping and green roofs; 3) Attenuate rainwater in ponds, swales or open water features for gradual release; 4) Attenuate rainwater by storing in tanks or sealed water features for gradual release; 5) Discharge into storm water collection system or watercourse (subject to license). Soakaways are suitable for single family dwelling houses but are not suitable for urban areas. (DM SWD 1)
water_environment.surface_water: Require that planning applications are accompanied by a comprehensive SUDs assessment that addresses run-off quantity, run-off quality and its impact on the existing habitat and water quality (SWD). Ensure that all storm water generated in a new development is disposed of on-site or is attenuated and treated prior to discharge to an approved storm water system. Promote storm water retention facilities ... in accordance with Sustainable Urban Drainage Systems as recommended in the Planning System and Flood Risk Management: Guidelines for Planning Authorities (DoEHLG, 2009) and Laois County Council's Roads and Drainage Standards. Development will not be allowed within 100m of a treatment works or 25m of a pumping station. This distance may be increased if significant environmental issues are likely to arise and will be judged on a site by site basis. The buffer area may be used to fulfil open space requirements. (DM WS 1)
water_environment.bin_refuse_storage: The Council will require that all developments include convenient and well-designed proposals for the storage of waste and recycling receptacles (3 receptacles per home). Secure pedestrian access shall be provided to the rear of terraced homes where appropriate. Sustainable waste reduction measures shall also be included in all proposals. (DM HS 11) Bring bank facilities will generally be required at appropriate locations ... One bring centre per residential development of 1,250 persons. (DM HS 12)
water_environment.daylight_sunlight: High buildings or when new buildings are located close to adjoining structures may significantly overshadow dwellings and open space. Daylight and shadow projection diagrams should be submitted for such proposals. The recommendations of 'Site Layout Planning for Daylight and Sunlight: A Guide to Good Practice', (B.R.E. 1991) or B.S. 8026 'Lighting for Buildings, Part 2 1992: Code of Practice for Day lighting' should be followed in this regard. (DM HS 8)
water_environment.noise: Specialised sporting facilities involving the use of motor vehicles, aircraft or firearms or other noise generating sports will be considered with the following criteria: There will be no unacceptable disturbance to local residents ... The ambient noise level in the area of the proposed development is already high and the noise likely to be produced by the proposed activity will not be pre-dominant ... (DM NR 1). For night clubs/disco-bars: Noise levels at the boundaries of these establishments will be monitored as circumstances require and mitigation measures will be required at the time of the submission of the planning application. (DM TC 2)
non_residential.retail_sequential_test: Ensure that the town and village centres of co. Laois retain their vitality and viability and that all future retail development is plan led in line with the settlement strategy (Ch.7 Aim). In assessing planning applications for commercial development a number of considerations will be taken into account: 1) Conformity with the land use policies in respect of commercial development ... Demolition within town centres will not normally be permitted unless fully justified by structural assessment and positive redevelopment proposals. (DM TC 1)
non_residential.retail_floorspace_caps: Require a Retail Impact Assessment to be carried out for development proposals in the following general circumstances: a) Proposals featuring greater than 1,000sqm of net floorspace for both convenience and comparison type developments in the Principal Town of Portlaoise; b) Proposals featuring greater than 500sqm of net retail floorspace for both convenience and comparison type developments in all other settlements; c) Or where the Planning Authority considers the development may impact on the vitality and viability of a town centre. The Retail Impact Assessment shall include, at minimum, the criteria set out in the Retail Planning Guidelines 2012. (DM TC 7) Petrol filling stations can include an associated shop (no more than 100 sq.ms. (net retail floorspace) that provides for the sale of convenience goods (DM TC 3).
non_residential.shopfront_signage: Generally, the Council will: 1) Seek the retention of traditional shopfronts; 2) Encourage the reinstatement of shopfronts, where they have fallen into disrepair; 3) Consider contemporarily-designed bespoke shopfronts in new commercial areas or on historic buildings if existing shopfronts are of poor quality; 4) Ensure new shop fronts provide ramped or level access to facilitate access for all. Traditional shopfronts: the stallriser at the base ... should occupy approximately a third of the height of the shopfront; the fascia board ... should not exceed approximately a fifth of the height of the shopfront. (DM TC 8)
non_residential.advertising: Shopfront advertisements (DM TC 9): ... Modern boxed fascia advertisements, which project from the face of the building, with internal illumination are not acceptable. Neon and fluorescent lighting is not acceptable ... The fascia board should normally state only the name or trade of the business and the street number ... Letters should generally not exceed 60 percent of the height of the fascia ... No more than one hanging sign per shopfront. The erection of speakers on the exterior of commercial or residential premises will not be permitted. Advertisements on public roads (DM TC 11): Within urban speed limits signs will not be permitted for businesses; Signs for rural businesses ... only be considered at an appropriate junction on a regional road and one sign at each junction leading to the business; Signs for tourism attractions and facilities will only be considered white on brown signs.
non_residential.employment_industry: Where proposed industrial facilities, waste disposal facilities, mineral extraction developments, or other developments that may have a significant impact on surface water quality, ground water quality, or on the water table, the developer will be required (whether as part of an EIAR or an Environmental Report) to submit an assessment of the impact of the development on water quality and the water table ... (DM ED 1 — Industrial Activities). Commercial development — warehouses and industrial uses/business campuses assessment criteria include nature/activities/processes, compatibility and impact with/on adjoining uses, traffic implications and sustainable mobility plans, quality of building design, site layout including car parking, landscaping plans, energy efficiency, surface water/stormwater management, climate resilience measures. (DM ED 2)
non_residential.home_based_economic: Home based economic activity is defined as 'working from home'. In dealing with applications ... the Planning Authority will have regard to: 1) The nature and extent of the work and the justification for the proposed location; 2) The effects on the amenities of adjoining properties particularly as regards hours of operation, noise and general disturbance; 3) The anticipated levels of traffic generation (a traffic and car parking statement shall accompany any application); 4) The details concerning the generation, storage and collection of waste; 5) Quality signage proposals — plastics and neon signage on buildings will not be permitted. Permissions for such partial change of use will be restricted to use by the applicant and may be temporary ... Permission will not normally be granted for such changes of use in apartments. (DM ED 3)
non_residential.agricultural_separation: In dealing with applications for agricultural developments the Planning Authority will have regard to the following: 1) Require that buildings be sited as unobtrusively as possible and that the finishes and colour used will blend the development into its surroundings ... 5) Appropriate roof colours are dark grey, dark reddish brown or a very dark green. Where cladding is used on the exterior of the farm buildings dark colours should be used ... All agricultural buildings should be located an adequate distance from any watercourse to reduce the risk of contamination. (DM RL 1) [No numeric separation distance from dwellings is stated.]
non_residential.petrol_stations: Applications for filling stations should have regard of the Retail Planning: Guidelines for Planning Authorities (DECLG, 2012) and the Spatial Planning and National Roads: Guidelines for Planning Authorities (NRA/TII, 2012) also take account of: 1) Be located within urban areas within speed limits; 2) Access to filling stations will not be permitted closer than 35 metres to a road junction; 3) Frontage on primary and secondary routes must be at least 20 metres in length; 4) All pumps and installations shall be set back at least 5 metres from the roads; 5) A wall, of a minimum height of 0.5 metres, must separate the forecourt from the public footpath ... 7) A proliferation of large illuminated projecting signs will not be permitted at filling stations - Generally only one such sign will be permitted ... 9) Petrol filling stations can include an associated shop (no more than 100 sq.ms. (net retail floorspace) ... 10) An undue concentration of filling stations shall not be permitted. (DM TC 3)
non_residential.hot_food_takeaway: The development of hot food take-aways will be strictly controlled and a proliferation of this use will not be encouraged. This type of development will generally only be considered in towns and villages where the development would not result in the loss of retail and office frontage. The Council may impose restrictions on opening hours. The opening of new fast food/takeaway outlets in close proximity to schools or children's play areas will be restricted ... standalone take-aways not attached to restaurants will not be encouraged ... Quality signage proposals, plastic or neon signage will not be permitted. (DM TC 6)
energy_infrastructure.wind_energy_setbac: Ensure a setback distance for Wind turbines from schools, dwellings, community centres and all public roads in all areas open for consideration for wind farm development as per the Guidelines for Planning Authorities on Wind Energy Development (Department of Housing, Planning and Local Government) (CM RE 8). Refer to Section 6 and Section 7 of Appendix 5 Wind Energy Strategy for the full suite of Development Management Standards (DM RE 2). [No numeric setback distance is stated in the chapter — deferred to the national Wind Energy Development Guidelines and Appendix 5.]
energy_infrastructure.solar: The following factors will be used to assess applications for Solar Farm Development within the county: (i) Site Selection: Preference for use of brownfield sites/contaminated land and non productive agricultural land versus productive agricultural lands; Proximity to electricity infrastructure. (ii) Assessment of Impacts: Effect of glint and glare; Visual impact on heritage and landscape assets ... Ecology ... Cumulative impacts ... Traffic impact ... Drainage, surface water runoff, flooding; Effect on potential archaeological heritage ... (iii) As a minimum: Drawings including drainage; Landscape/Biodiversity Plan; Construction Environmental Management Plan; and Decommissioning/Restoration plan. (DM RE 1)
energy_infrastructure.telecommunications: To facilitate the evaluation of development proposals for the erection of antennae and support structures, applicants will be required to: 1) Submit a reasoned justification as to the need ... ensuring that ComReg's Code of Conduct is implemented; 2) Indicate what other sites or locations in the County were considered; 3) Submit evidence of consultations ... with regard to the sharing of sites ... 6) Furnish a statement of compliance with the International Radiation Protection Association (IRPA) Guidelines ... 10) ... monopoles are preferred to latticework types ... should be of dull or neutral sky grey colour ... Green or black is the preferred colour at ground level ... 14) ... submit either a Discovery Series Map or similar ... to the scale of 1:50,000 the location of all telecommunication structures within a radius of 1km of the proposed site. Have regard to Telecommunications Antennae and Support Structures Guidelines for Planning Authorities (DoELG, 1996) and Circular Letter PL07/12. (DM TEL 1)
energy_infrastructure.overhead_lines: Applicants shall ensure that planning applications involving the siting of power lines and other overhead cables fully consider the following: i. Impacts on the landscape, national monuments, archaeology and views of special amenity value. Where impacts are inevitable, mitigation measures to minimise such impact must be provided; ii. Impacts on Ecology — An ecological Impact assessment shall be submitted to inform the decision making process; iii. Development shall be consistent with best practice, with regard to siting and design. (DM NRE 1) In new housing and in other forms of urban development, all services including E.S.B., telephone and television cables shall be [located underground] (DM TEL 2 — Undergrounding and Concealing of Services).
heritage_natural.archaeology_zones: In areas of archaeological potential, where groundworks are proposed, the Council favours the preservation in-situ of archaeological remains ... Where it is proposed to undertake groundworks to lands within an area of archaeological potential or in the vicinity of Recorded Monuments or Zones of Archaeological Potential, the Council will require the preparation of an archaeological field evaluation by a licensed archaeologist, the details of which will be submitted with a planning application. Such development shall be assessed in the context of the Framework and Principles for the Protection of Archaeological Heritage (DoAHG, 1999), the National Monuments Acts 1934-1994, and the Heritage Council's Archaeology and Development Guidelines for Good Practice for Developers (2000). (DM AH 1)
heritage_natural.protected_structures_cu: In considering applications for development within the curtilage and/or attendant grounds of a protected structure, the Council shall have regard to: the various elements of the structure which give the protected structure its special character ...; the proximity of any new development to the main protected structure ...; the design of the new development that should relate to and complement the special character of the protected structure; Outward and inward views from the protected structure are to be protected. High quality design will be a foremost consideration ... with particular emphasis on siting, building lines, proportions, scale, massing, height, roof treatment and materials. (DM PS 1) Extensions to vernacular structures: Extensions which break the existing front building line will not normally be acceptable ... Extensions are subordinate to the existing dwelling with a presumption against the size of any extension exceeding 100% of the floor area of the existing dwelling. (DM VS 1)
heritage_natural.biodiversity_green_infr: Require all proposals for large scale development such as road or drainage schemes, wind farms, housing estates, industrial parks or shopping centres to submit a Green Infrastructure Plan as an integral part of a planning application (DM BNH 3). Where there are trees within an application site, or on land adjacent ... the planning application must include a detailed submission prepared by a suitably qualified Arboriculturist in accordance with British Standard 5837: 2012 ... A Tree Management Plan shall be provided (DM BNH 4). There will be a firm presumption against the removal of hedgerows to facilitate development including where sight lines are deficient. In cases where removal is necessary, the planting of an equivalent length of native hedgerow will be required ... developers should be reminded of their obligations under the Wildlife Acts not to remove or interfere with them during the bird nesting season, between March 1st and 31st August (DM BNH 5). All applications for residential developments shall include a Landscape and Biodiversity Plans ... The use of native trees, shrubs and plants in the planting schedules ... Beech (fagus sylvatica) shall not be used (DM HS 19).

rural_site_scaling: Larger houses (e.g. in excess of 200 m²) should sub-divide mass into smaller elements
sightline_x_m: 3.0 m back from edge of carriageway
sightline_y: National Primary & R280 215/215/80 m; Regionally & Locally Important 215/160/80 m; Other Regional N-A/160/80 m; County Roads N-A/120*/70 m (100/80/60 km/h)
wastewater_standard: EPA Code of Practice for Domestic Waste Water Treatment Systems (p.e. <10) (2021)
residential_density_dph: infill ~15 units/ha, edge of centre ~30, outer suburban ~25; Tier 2A Growth Towns 20; Key Villages 15; lower-density settlements 5-10 (Table 2.6)
site_coverage_pct: Residential 50%; Industrial 75%; Other employment 60%; Retail & commercial 66%; Town centre up to 80%
separation_distance_m: 22 m between opposing above-ground-floor / first-floor habitable room windows; 2.0-2.5 m between detached and semi-detached dwellings
private_open_space: 1/2 bed: 55 m²; 3 bed: 60 m²; 4+ bed: 75 m² (Table 13.3)
public_open_space_pct: Greenfield min 15%; large infill/brownfield min 10%; institutional lands 20%
car_parking_dwelling: 2 per unit (car) / 1 per unit (cycle)
building_height: Outside Carrick-on-Shannon limited to 3-4 storeys; greater-than-prevailing needs urban design rationale
trigger_AA: Habitats Directive Art 6(3); Natura Impact Statement where AA Screening concludes AA required (§13.2.5)
trigger_EIA: EIA Directive transposed Section 5 Parts 1 & 2 PD Regs 2001; EIA where threshold met or screening sub-threshold; culminates in EIAR (§13.2.6)
trigger_FRA: Site-specific FRA where flooding may be an issue, per Planning System and Flood Risk Management Guidelines (2009) (§13.2.7)
trigger_AHIA: Architectural Heritage Assessment may be required (§13.2.9)
trigger_EcIA: EcIA where development likely to significantly impact flora/fauna/habitats; per CIEEM (2019) (§13.2.8)
trigger_VIA: Visual Impact Assessment / Landscape Character Impact Assessment may be required (§13.2.9)
trigger_TTA: Traffic and Transport Assessment may be required (§13.2.9)
residential.housing_mix: Apartment developments may include up to 50% one-bedroom or studio type units (with no more than 20-25% of the total proposed development as studios) and there shall be no minimum requirement for apartments with three or more bedrooms (in accordance with SPPR 1 of the Guidelines). For all building refurbishment schemes on sites of any size, or urban infill schemes on sites of up to 0.25ha: Where up to 9 residential units are proposed, notwithstanding SPPR 1 of the guidelines, there shall be no restriction on dwelling mix, provided that no more than 50% of the development (i.e. up to 4 units) comprises studio-type units. Where between 10 to 49 residential units are proposed, the flexible dwelling mix provision for the first 9 units may be carried forward and the parameters set out in SPPR 1, shall apply from the 10th residential unit to the 49th. For schemes of 50 or more units, SPPR 1 shall apply to the entire development. (Ref: SPPR 2).
residential.dwelling_min_floor_areas: The following guidance documents should be consulted (unless otherwise updated) when designing a residential development: 'The Guidelines on Quality Housing for Sustainable Communities', DEHLG, (2007); 'The Guidelines for Planning Authorities on Sustainable Residential Development in Urban Areas', DEHLG, (2009).
residential.apartment_min_floor_areas: Applications for apartment developments will be assessed having regard to 'The Design Standards for New Apartments' (DoHPLG, 2018). Table 13.4 Minimum Area Standards for Apartments — Floor Area: Studio 37 m²; One Bedroom 45 m²; Two Bedroom (3 persons) 63 m²; Two Bedroom (4 persons) 73 m²; Three Bedroom 90 m². For apartment schemes of 10 or more units, the majority of apartments must exceed the minimum floor area standard for any combination of the relevant 1, 2 or 3-bedroom unit types, by a minimum of 10% (in accordance with SPPR 3).
residential.apartment_dual_aspect_pct: In terms of dual aspect provisions (in accordance with SPPR 4): A minimum of 33% of dual aspect units will be required in more central and accessible urban locations, where it is necessary to achieve a quality design in response to the site characteristics and ensure good street frontage where appropriate. In suburban or intermediate locations it is an objective that there shall generally be a minimum of 50% dual aspect apartments in a single scheme. For building refurbishment schemes on sites of any size or urban infill schemes on sites of up to 0.25ha, further discretion may be exercised to consider dual aspect unit provision at a level lower than the 33% minimum outlined above on a case-by-case basis.
residential.apartment_storage: Table 13.4 Minimum Area Standards for Apartments — Storage Area: Studio 3 m²; One Bedroom 3 m²; Two Bedroom (3 persons) 5 m²; Two Bedroom (4 persons) 6 m²; Three Bedroom 9 m².
residential.apartment_floor_to_ceiling: Ground level apartment floor to ceiling heights shall be a minimum of 2.7m and shall be increased in certain circumstances, particularly where necessary to facilitate a future change of use to a commercial use. For building refurbishment schemes on sites of any size or urban infill schemes on sites of up to 0.25ha, the Planning Authority may exercise discretion on a case-by-case basis, subject to overall design quality (in accordance with SPPR 5). A maximum of 12 apartments per floor per core may be provided in apartment schemes (in accordance with SPPR 6).
residential.extensions: Proposals, which involve the alteration, improvement, or extension of an existing dwelling should reflect and enhance the existing building, adjoining properties, and the setting in terms of scale, design and materials. The extension should be sensitive to the existing dwelling in its form, scale and appearance and should not adversely distort the scale or mass of the structure or of adjoining properties. The extension should not provide for new overlooking of the private area of an adjacent residence where no such overlooking previously existed. New extensions should not overshadow adjacent dwellings to the degree that there is a significant decrease in daylight or sunlight entering the house. In all cases a minimum private rear garden area must be retained in accordance with Table 13.3. (13.10.10)
residential.family_flat_max_gfa: Family flat or 'granny' flat accommodation represents a temporary arrangement to accommodate an immediate family member or carer within an existing dwelling unit in a semi-independent capacity. The proposed unit should be linked directly to the main dwelling by a connecting door. Accommodation must be subsidiary to the main dwelling in scale and only in exceptional cases will more than one bedroom be permitted. The design shall ensure that the flat can easily fully revert to being part of the original house when no longer occupied by family member(s). It shall be normal procedure to include a condition or conditions in any grant of permission that the family flat cannot be sold, conveyed or leased separately from the main residence, and that when the need for the family flat no longer exists that the dwelling must be returned to a single dwelling unit. (13.10.11) — no maximum gross floor area figure stated.
residential.domestic_garage_pod: Domestic Garage/Shed/Store Development (applicable in both urban and rural settings): The design, form and materials should be ancillary to, and consistent with the main dwelling on site. Structures should generally be detached and sited to the side or rear of the dwelling house and be visually subservient in terms of size, scale, and bulk. Storage facilities should be used solely for purposes incidental to the enjoyment of the dwelling and not for any commercial, manufacturing, industrial use, or habitable space in the absence of prior planning consent for such use. (13.11.1)
residential.boundary_treatment_height: High quality boundary treatments are generally required to enclose private open space. A 1.8m – 2m high wall of solid block, capped and plastered on both sides, is generally acceptable. Concrete post and base with timber panel fencing will be considered for the side boundaries between rear gardens, provided a 2m length of 1.8-2m high block wall, capped and plastered, is provided for the initial 2m from the rear building line of the house. Two metre high screen walls should be provided between all areas of public space and rear gardens. In the interest of passive surveillance, where side boundary walls adjoin the public footpath, the walls shall be a maximum of 1.2 metre in height as far as the front building line of the dwelling (beyond which a 2m wall shall be provided). Boundaries between the rear of existing and proposed dwellings shall be a minimum of 1.8m high. (13.10.4)
residential.naming: Names of residential and other developments shall reflect local heritage by incorporating local place names or names of geographical, historical or cultural significance to the site location. Names of developments shall include the use of the Irish language. In order to ensure the above and to avoid confusion and duplication, the naming of developments will require the prior agreement of the Planning Authority. (13.10.8)
residential.taking_in_charge: To ensure the satisfactory completion of development works (roads, footpaths, surface water drainage, car parks, public lighting, open space, landscaping, including the protection of trees) undertaken by private developers a cash deposit or a bond from an insurance company or other financial institution acceptable to the Planning Authority will be required. Residential developments completed to a standard that is compliant with the planning permission granted and to the satisfaction of the Council will be taken in charge in accordance with Leitrim County Council's Taking in Charge Policy and the provisions of the Planning & Development Act 2000, as amended and the Roads Act 1993 upon receipt of a formal request for same. Where Management Companies are required for apartment schemes, a properly constituted Management Company shall be established. (13.10.9)
residential.bonds: To ensure that developments undertaken by private developers are satisfactorily completed, developers will be required to give cash deposits or submit a bond from an insurance company or other financial institution acceptable to the Council, for the satisfactory completion of developments and their ancillary services. This bond or security must be adequate to secure the completion of the development and must be in place before development is commenced. (13.6)
open_space.public_open_space_pct: Public open space on greenfield sites equating to a minimum of 15% of the total site area, a minimum 10% on large infill sites/brownfield sites and 20% on institutional lands. The areas of public open space shall be clearly identified on the site layout/landscaping plan. (13.10.7)
open_space.public_open_space_qual: Public open spaces should be appropriately located, passively overlooked by as many dwellings as possible and easily accessible to all residents, providing for both passive and active uses. Narrow tracts of land (less than 10 metres in width) are not acceptable. Areas of public open space should be generally flat. SuDS are not generally acceptable as a form of public open space provision, except where they contribute in a significant and positive way to the design and quality of open space; in general a maximum of 10% of the open space provision shall be taken up by SuDS. (13.10.7)
open_space.private_open_space_house: Table 13.3: Minimum Private Open Space Requirements for Dwelling House Types — One/Two Bedroom 55 m²; Three Bedroom 60 m²; Four Bedroom or more 75 m². Exceptions to the above minimum private open space requirements may be only considered in relation to the redevelopment of inner urban infill sites and the redevelopment of brownfield/regeneration sites. (13.10.5)
open_space.balcony_min_areas: Table 13.4: Minimum Area Standards for Apartments — Private Open Space: Studio 4 m²; One Bedroom 5 m²; Two Bedroom (3 persons) 6 m²; Two Bedroom (4 persons) 7 m²; Three Bedroom 9 m². Communal amenity space should be provided, suitable for passive recreation and including play spaces for smaller children. (13.10.6)
open_space.play_space: Public and semi-private open space in all residential developments will be required to incorporate natural features that promote children's play. Opportunities for children's play should be addressed as part of the landscaping plan. On large sites, areas should be identified for a hierarchy of uses e.g. casual 'pocket parks' for smaller children to play, informal kick about areas, areas for passive amenity. etc. (13.10.7)
community.childcare_provision: All childcare facilities shall be provided in accordance with the guidance, 'Childcare Facilities: Guidelines for Planning Authorities' (DoEHLG, 2001) and Circular Letter PL3 2016 or any superseding guidelines. In the assessment of individual planning applications for childcare facilities, the Planning Authority will have regard to: the justification for proposed site location; details on accessibility, including pedestrian, cycle and public transport provisions; adequate and safe set-down and collection areas; adequate on-site car parking for staff; adequate play area(s) for children attending. Large scale proposals may be required to present a Mobility Management Plan. (13.14.2)
community.education: Applications for educational provision shall comply with the requirements of technical guidance documents published by the Department of Education and Skills including 'Provision of Schools and the Planning System: A Code of Practice for Planning Authorities' (DoES, DoEHLG, 2008). The Council will have regard to: site location, proximity of school to catchment area, size of site relative to outdoor space requirements and the future needs of the school; traffic and transport impact; good, safe accessible pedestrian and cyclist routes; safe vehicular access and adequate car parking layout to facilitate drop off/pick up. Temporary school structures will generally be accepted subject to the development not impacting unduly on on-site car parking/cycle parking provision. (13.14.3)
community.health: Planning applications should include details of proposed professional medical activities, proposed number of practitioners and support staff, parking provision and intended hours of operation. The location of New Health Centres/Services, Primary Health Centres, Medical Consultants and Veterinary Surgeries is favoured in towns and villages with good accessibility. However new facilities in other locations or a change of use from residential to health facilities will be considered where the privacy and amenity of adjacent occupiers is preserved, and the proposal does not have a detrimental impact on local amenity by way of traffic congestion or noise. Adequate car parking/set down areas must be identified. (13.14.4)
transport.car_parking_standards: Table 13.6 establishes maximum car parking standards. Dwelling House 2 per unit; Apartment/Flat 1.5 per unit; Hotels/B&B/Guesthouse/Hostel 1 per bedroom; Food Retail 1 per 20m² GFA; Non-Food Retail 6 per 100m² GFA; Retail Warehousing 1 per 35m² of net retail floorspace; Banks and Financial Institutions 1 per 20m² GFA; Conference Centre 1 per 25m²; Cash and Carry 1 per 100m²; Warehousing 1 per 100m²; Bars/Function Rooms/lounges 1 per 10m²; Restaurants 1 per 20m² GFA; Café 1 per 10m² dining area; Takeaway 1 per 18m² GFA; Garages/Service Stations 1 per fuel pump plus 1 per 10m² of net retail floor area; Car Showrooms 1 per 50m² GFA; Garden Centres/Factory Outlets 2 per 100m² GFA; Cinema 1 space per 10 seats; Offices (Town Centre) 1 per 25m² of GFA; Offices (Bus Park/other) 1 per 20m² of GFA; Industry 1 per 60m² of GFA. Minimum size for a car parking space shall be 2.5 m x 5 m with circulation aisles of minimum 6 m in width; parallel to kerb 6 m x 2.5 m; disabled parking bay minimum 3 m in width; loading bays minimum 3 m x 6 m for small vans and 15 m x 3 m for HGVs (13.16.8). Disabled parking: 1 space for the first 25 standard spaces; minimum 3 where up to 50 spaces; minimum 5 where between 50-100 spaces (13.16.9).
transport.cycle_parking: Table 13.6 establishes minimum cycle parking standards. Dwelling House 1 per unit; Apartment/Flat 1 per bedroom/studio; Hotels/B&B/Guesthouse/Hostel 1 per 20 beds; Hostel 1 per 10 beds; Food Retail 1 per 75m²; Non-Food Retail 1 per 100m²; Retail Warehousing 1 per 500m²; Banks and Financial Institutions 1 per 50m²; Conference Centre 1 per 20 seats; Cash and Carry 1 per 150m²; Warehousing 1 per 500m²; Bars/Function Rooms/lounges 1 per 75m²; Restaurants 1 per 75m²; Café 1 per 50m²; Takeaway Nil; Garages/Service Stations 1 per 50m²; Car Showrooms Nil; Garden Centres/Factory Outlets 1 per 150m²; Cinema 1 per 20 seats; Offices (Town Centre) 1 per 5 employees; Offices (Bus Park/other) 1 per 10 employees; Industry 1 per 500m². Showers — Office developments: 250–500 m² 1 shower; 500 to 1,000 m² 2 showers; 1 shower per additional 1,000 m² thereafter. Non-office: 1 shower for the first 5 cycle parking spaces and 1 shower per 10 cycle parking spaces thereafter. (13.16.7)
transport.ev_charging_pct: All developments should provide facilities for the charging of battery-operated cars at a rate of up to 20% of the total car parking spaces. The remainder of the parking spaces should be constructed (wiring and ducting provided) so as to be capable of accommodating future charging points. New residential development should accommodate at least one car parking space equipped with two EV charging points for every ten no. car parking spaces being provided. (13.16.10)
transport.building_line: In the case of two or more industrial/warehouse units, a uniform design is required for boundary treatments, roof profiles and building lines. The front building line shall be as determined in consultation with the Planning Authority and, where required, the existing roadside boundary shall be set back (13.12.2). For business parks: Building lines shall generally respect the existing established pattern. Undeveloped areas shall provide a minimum of 15 m setback along principal road frontages with appropriate (minimum 5 m depth) landscaping to the front of the buildings (13.12.3).
transport.stopping_distances_sightlines: All new entrances and junctions will require clear and unobstructed sight lines in accordance with Table 13.5: National Primary Roads & R280 215 m (100 km/h)/215 m (80 km/h)/80 m (60 km/h or less); Regionally & Locally Important Roads 215/160/80 m; Other Regional Roads N/A/160/80 m; County Roads N/A/120 m*/70 m (*except where operational speed is 60kph or less, 90m may be considered satisfactory). Sight distances are measured at a height of 1.05 m above ground level from a point 3.0 m back from the edge of the carriageway to a point on the road 260 mm over the surface level of the road. Distance of entrances from road junctions (rural): National Primary Roads 150 metres; National Secondary Roads 100 metres; Regional Roads 100 metres; Local Primary Roads 40 metres. (13.16.4 / 13.16.5)
transport.universal_access: The Council encourages that all new development proposals be designed in accordance with the standards outlined in the 'National Disability Authority - Building for Everyone; A Universal Design Approach 2012' and Technical Guidance Document M - Access and Use of the Building Regulations 2010 or any subsequent update to these standards. (13.2.2)
transport.loading_coach_taxi: In addition to car parking standards, sufficient space will be required within the curtilage of a site for all service vehicles involved in the operation of the business or building. Set down areas and/or bus circulation areas may also be required depending on the nature of the proposed development. Loading bays shall be a minimum 3 m x 6 m for small vans and 15 m x 3 m for HGVs. (13.16.8)
water_environment.suds_requirement: All new developments (including amendments/extensions to existing developments) will be required to incorporate 'Sustainable Urban Drainage Systems' (SuDS) as part of the development/design proposals, where feasible. SuDS include devices such as swales, permeable pavements, filter drains, storage ponds, constructed wetlands, soakways and green roofs. In some exceptional cases, where it is demonstrated that SuDS devices are not feasible, approval may be given to install underground attenuation tanks or enlarged pipes in conjunction with other devices, only as a last resort. Proposals for surface water attenuation systems should include maintenance proposals and demonstrate that the rate of discharge equates to the pre development greenfield run off rate. Development proposals will be required to be accompanied by a comprehensive SuDS assessment that addresses run-off rate, run-off quality and its impact on the existing habitat and water quality. (13.17.4)
water_environment.surface_water: Surface water shall not be permitted to flow on to the public road from any rural residential site. Accesses and road frontage should be designed in such a manner as to deal with surface water and ensure that it does not impact on the public road. Existing roadside drainage shall be maintained by the incorporation of a suitably sized drainage pipe. Each application shall be accompanied by design calculations or appropriate evidence to support the size of the pipe selected (13.11). All new developments will be required to utilise and connect to the piped water and wastewater network, operated by Irish Water, where practicable. (13.17.1)
water_environment.bin_refuse_storage: Adequate provision shall be made for the storage (3 wheelie bins) and collection of waste materials. Terraced/townhouses shall provide appropriately located screened bin storage locations which shall be identified in the planning application. Where communal bin facilities are being provided, they shall be conveniently located, screened, and well ventilated. (13.10.3). Bring bank facilities: the provision of an area of at least 10 m by 4 m; truck access and clearance heights; a hard standing area and safe pedestrian walkway; a vehicle set down area only with no permanent parking provision; suitable lighting and CCTV monitoring (13.17.8).
water_environment.daylight_sunlight: Where development of a significant height is located close to existing development, the Planning Authority may require daylight and shadow projection diagrams to be submitted. The recommendations of 'Site Layout Planning for Daylight and Sunlight: A Guide to Good Practice' (BRE 1991) or 'Lighting for Buildings Part 2 1992: Code of Practice for Day Lighting B.S. 8206' and any updates to these documents should be followed as a minimum in this regard. (13.9.5)
water_environment.noise: Noise levels shall generally not exceed 55 dB (a) Leq when measured at the boundary of the site where such noise would result in an adverse impact on amenity to an adjoining sensitive land use such as residential development (industrial development, 13.12.2). For fast food outlets, noise insulation measures will be required at the time of the submission of the planning application and ongoing noise monitoring may be required (13.13.2). External floodlighting shall have fully-shielded light fixtures and cowled; reference should be made to BS EN 12193:2007 Light and Lighting – Sports Lighting (13.14.7).
non_residential.retail_sequential_test: Retail development should be in accordance with the fundamental objective to support the vitality and viability of the retail centre. All applications for retail developments at edge-of-centre or out-of-centre locations will be subject to the sequential test. The provision of new retail development shall be in accordance with Chapter 4 of this Plan, the 'Retail Planning Guidelines' and the accompanying 'Retail Design Manual' (DoECLG, 2012). Retail impact assessment may be required for significant retail development which due to their scale and/or location may impact on the vitality and viability of town centres. (13.13)
non_residential.retail_floorspace_caps: All retail proposals exceeding 1,000 m² shall provide a Transport Impact Assessment (TIA) in addition to any other information deemed necessary by the Planning Authority (13.13). Service Stations: The retail floor space of the shop shall not exceed 100 m² net; where permission is sought for a floor space in excess of 100 m² net, the sequential approach to retail development shall apply in accordance with the 'Retail Planning Guidelines', DECLG (2012) (13.13.1).
non_residential.shopfront_signage: The design of shop fronts should reflect the scale and proportions of the existing streetscape. The Council will seek to retain the remaining traditional shop fronts of townscape importance. The replacement of an existing shopfront, or the insertion of a new shopfront requires planning permission. Applications for new shop fronts shall have regard to: detailed plans at a scale of 1:50; all signage must be compatible with the existing streetscape (timber, stone, glass and steel are preferred materials); the twin elements of a fascia board and pilasters shall be provided in all cases. The Council will discourage the use of external roller shutters and their boxes and projecting brand signs on the exterior of shop-fronts. Where roller shutters are proposed they must be internal and located behind the display window. (13.13.5 / 13.13.6)
non_residential.advertising: Outdoor advertising structures, including trailer type structures, will not generally be permitted within the county, whether freestanding or attached to buildings. In particular, the use of gables or sides of buildings for the exhibition of advertising structures will not normally be permitted. Billboards or similar types of advertisements may be permitted in limited circumstances for a specified time period, where it can be demonstrated that the development will enhance the area e.g. by screening an unsightly site or derelict structure. The use of freestanding signs/advertising boards on or over the public footpath will not be permitted. Signage on National Roads will be strictly controlled and will generally be only permitted in accordance with the provisions set out in Section 3.8 of the Spatial Planning and National Roads Guidelines (2012). Finger Post Signage: only the primary route to the facility shall be signed; the sign should be located within 2km of the facility or relevant junction. (13.13.7 / 13.13.8)
non_residential.employment_industry: Industrial and warehousing development schemes will be required to present a good quality appearance. Individual buildings should exhibit a high quality of modern architectural design and finish. Storage and stacking areas shall be located to the rear of the building or, where such facilities are located at the side, provision for screening shall be made. A landscaped buffer zone (minimum 5-10 metres) will be a requirement of planning permissions for any industrial/warehousing development where it adjoins an area with another land use zoning objective or where it would impact on the amenities of adjoining land uses. The hours of industrial operation will be controlled where they are likely to result in harm to environmental amenities. Noise levels shall generally not exceed 55 dB (a) Leq when measured at the boundary of the site. (13.12.2)
non_residential.business_tech_parks: Business parks shall be laid out in open parkland setting with a high level of landscaping, and provision shall be made for pedestrian and cycle paths throughout. For all significant employment developments comprising schemes with a gross floor area of 1,000 m² and above, a Design Statement will be required. Building lines shall generally respect the existing established pattern. Undeveloped areas shall provide a minimum of 15 m setback along principal road frontages with appropriate (minimum 5 m depth) landscaping to the front of the buildings. A Workplace Travel Plan will be required for a large-scale employment generating development that gives rise to significant demand in trips. (13.12.3)
non_residential.home_based_economic: Home-based economic activity is defined as small scale commercial activity carried out by a resident of a house which is subordinate to the use of the dwelling as a place of residence. The Planning Authority will have regard to: the nature and extent of the work; the effects on the amenities of adjoining properties particularly as regards hours of operation, availability of car parking, noise and general disturbance; the anticipated levels of traffic generation; and the generation, storage and collection of waste. Permissions for home-based economic activity will generally be restricted to use by the applicant only in a rural area. Applications may be subject to a temporary permission. (13.12.4)
non_residential.agricultural_separation: Agricultural buildings, structures and associated works should relate to the landscape and be sympathetic to their surroundings in scale, materials and finishes. Traditionally this was achieved by having the roof darker than the walls. The grouping of agricultural buildings is encouraged in order to reduce their overall impact. In the construction and layout of agricultural buildings, the Council will require: that buildings are sited appropriately in order to minimise obtrusion on the landscape and will generally seek to cluster agricultural buildings and structures together; use of dark coloured cladding (dark browns, greys, greens and reds); satisfactory treatment of effluents, including yard run-off within the confines of the site; compliance with the European Union (Good Agricultural Practice for Protection of Waters) (Amendment) Regulations 2014; and traffic safety considerations. (13.18.1) — no numeric separation distance stated.
non_residential.seveso: Planning Authorities are required to have regard to the potential effects of relevant development in terms of the risks or consequences of a major accident for public health and safety. Planning applications pertaining to or in proximity to Seveso sites are referred to the Health and Safety Authority (HSA) for their input. The Planning Authority will consider the need to maintain an appropriate safe distance of such establishments from residential areas, areas of public use, and areas of particular natural sensitivity. For some types of development, particularly those involving large numbers of people and vulnerable occupants, the Planning Authority will consider the societal risk. Developers of Seveso sites and any sites adjacent to Seveso sites are encouraged to consult with the HSA at the pre-planning stage. (13.21) — no numeric consultation distances stated.
non_residential.petrol_stations: Proposals for new and extended service stations will be carefully considered and will not generally be encouraged within the core retail area of urban centres or in the open countryside outside of towns and villages. The forecourt canopy should be integrated into the overall design and sited so that it does not dominate the surrounding buildings. The retail floor space of the shop shall not exceed 100 m² net; where permission is sought for a floor space in excess of 100 m² net, the sequential approach to retail development shall apply in accordance with the 'Retail Planning Guidelines', DECLG (2012). EV charging point(s) should be provided and clearly demarcated. Proposals for new on-line or off-line motorway service facilities will be assessed in accordance with the guidance set out in the Spatial Planning and National Roads Guidelines for Planning Authorities, DECLG (2012). (13.13.1)
non_residential.amusement_arcades: In assessing planning applications for Secondary Retail Type Outlets (Including Betting Office/Amusement Arcades/Casino/Tattoo Parlour/Vaping Shops), the following considerations will be taken into account: the need to safeguard the vitality and viability of the defined retail area and to maintain a suitable mix of retail uses; the number/frequency of such facilities in the area; the effect on the amenities in the area arising from noise, hours of operation and litter; the treatment of shop front advertising and window display. (13.13.3)
non_residential.hot_food_takeaway: In order to maintain the appropriate mix of uses and protect the amenities in a particular area, it is the policy of the Council to prevent the excessive concentration of fast food outlets and take-aways. The provision of such outlets will be assessed having regard to: the number and frequency of such facilities in an area and their cumulative impact; the need to safeguard the vitality and viability of shopping areas in the town centre; the proximity of such uses to other vulnerable uses, e.g. residences, schools, open space; the likely impact on general and residential amenity in terms of noise/disturbance, traffic, parking, litter, and fumes; proposed façade design; proposed opening hours must be specified and these will be controlled by the Planning Authority. Noise insulation measures will be required. (13.13.2)
non_residential.vape_betting: In assessing planning applications for Secondary Retail Type Outlets (Including Betting Office/Amusement Arcades/Casino/Tattoo Parlour/Vaping Shops), the following considerations will be taken into account: the need to safeguard the vitality and viability of the defined retail area and to maintain a suitable mix of retail uses; the number/frequency of such facilities in the area; the effect on the amenities in the area arising from noise, hours of operation and litter; the treatment of shop front advertising and window display. (13.13.3)
energy_infrastructure.wind_energy_setbac: When assessing planning applications for wind energy developments, the Council will have regard to the 'Wind Energy Development Guidelines for Planning Authorities' (DoEHLG, 2006), and any updates to these Guidelines which may be made, and the 'Interim Guidelines for Planning Authorities on Statutory Plans, Renewable Energy and Climate Change' (DoHPCLG, 2017). Considerations include: consideration of falling distance plus an additional flashover distance from wind turbines to overhead transmission lines; noise disturbance (including consistency with the World Health Organisation's 2018 Environmental Noise Guidelines for the European Region); shadow flicker (for wind turbine developments, including detailed Shadow Flicker Study). (13.20.1) — no fixed numeric setback distance stated.
energy_infrastructure.solar: The Council will consider applications for solar renewable energy development having regard to the following criteria: any future Section 28 Ministerial Guidance; site suitability; any environmental sensitivities; Landscape Character Areas of the county; visual impact, zones of influence including cumulative visual impact of existing/permitted solar farms; Glint and Glare impacts on roads, dwellings, national monuments, Protected Structures and other sensitive receptors; the need to protect the existing level of residential amenities; Archaeological Impact Assessment and Heritage Impact Assessment; Ecological Impact Assessment; landscaping plans; security requirements; impacts from lighting; construction impacts; impacts on drainage patterns and water tables; suitability of and access to the electricity grid; and a Decommissioning Statement. (13.20.2)
energy_infrastructure.telecommunications: Proposals for telecommunications antennae and support structures will be assessed in accordance with the 'Telecommunications Antennae and Support Structures – Guidelines for Planning Authorities' (DoELG, 1996), as revised by Circular Letter PL 07/12. Planning applications for new facilities should include: a reasoned justification regarding the need for the particular development at the proposed location; details of efforts made to share installations or co-locate/cluster with existing structures, referencing a map showing the location of all existing structures within a minimum 2km radius of the proposed site; evidence of consideration of alternative sites; visual impact assessment and mitigation measures; and any impacts on rights of way and walking. (13.20.3)
energy_infrastructure.overhead_lines: Where undergrounding of cables is being pursued, proposals should demonstrate that environmental impacts including the following are minimised: habitat loss as a result of removal of field boundaries and hedgerows; short to medium term impacts on the landscape; impacts on underground archaeology; impacts on soil structure and drainage; and impacts on surface waters as a result of sedimentation. For wind energy: consideration of falling distance plus an additional flashover distance from wind turbines to overhead transmission lines. (13.20.4 / 13.20.1)
heritage_natural.archaeology_zones: When considering development proposals within Zones of Archaeological Potential and on, or in close proximity to, sites of known archaeological significance, the Council will have regard to the provisions of Section 12 of the National Monuments (Amendment) Act, 1994 (as amended). The Council may require the developer to submit a report prepared by a suitably qualified archaeologist on the archaeological implications of the proposed development. In appropriate circumstances, the Council when granting permission may impose conditions requiring: professional archaeological supervision of site excavations; the funding by the applicant of further archaeological assessment, monitoring, testing or excavation; and the preservation of all or part of any archaeological remains on the site. (13.15.4)
heritage_natural.protected_structures_cu: In assessing proposals for development affecting a Protected Structure, the Council will require planning permission for works, both to the exterior and interior, which materially affects the character of a Protected Structure. When making a planning application for permission to restore, refurbish, demolish, develop or change of use of Protected Structures, the Council will normally require the submission of an Architectural Assessment Report as described in Appendix B of the 'Architectural Heritage Protection, Guidelines for Planning Authorities' (DoEHLG, 2004). For ACAs: new alterations and extensions should complement existing buildings/structures in terms of design, external finishes, colour, texture, windows/doors/roof/chimney/design. All works to Protected Structures shall be carried out in accordance with best conservation practice. (13.15.2 / 13.15.3)
heritage_natural.biodiversity_green_infr: Development proposals should consider potential ecological impacts on the natural heritage and 'Green Infrastructure' present within or adjacent to the site, including Special Areas of Conservation and Special Protection Areas, Salmonid Waters and Freshwater Pearl Mussel catchments, and green infrastructure including features of the landscape that provide linkages/connectivity to designated sites. Where a development is of a significant scale or in close proximity to a pNHA, the Council will require an Ecological Impact Assessment (EcoIA). All planning applications shall be screened for Appropriate Assessment. Where the removal of hedges/trees during development is proposed, a detailed replanting proposal shall provide for the replacement of, at minimum, an equal amount of similar indigenous hedgerows and the planting of a minimum of five no. mature/established trees per tree felled. All trees with stem girth of 75mm and height above 1.5m from ground level should be included in tree/hedgerow surveys. (13.15.1 / 13.9.6)

ribbon_development_rule: In order to limit the negative visual impact arising from a series of dwellings along a road in rural areas, dwellings generally should avoid regular minimum set back and be staggered in relation to adjacent development, subject to the amenities of nearby residents being protected and no adverse effect on carrying capacity and traffic safety. Note: Suburban-type and/or ribbon development is not acceptable in rural areas as set out in the Sustainable Rural Guidelines and any subsequent update.
road_setback_national_m: National Primary and National Secondary Roads 20 m.; New National Primary Roads 90 m. (Table DM 7)
road_setback_regional_m: County Roads and Regional Roads 30 m. (minimum building line from the near road edge, Table DM 7)
sightline_y: Petrol stations: <80 km/h 160 m min; ≥80 km/h 215 m min
wastewater_standard: Rural one-off housing per EPA 2021 Code of Practice; site assessment by registered site assessor
residential_density_dph: Serviced sites: generally 10 housing units per hectare
separation_distance_m: 22 m between opposing windows (apartments up to 3 storeys); 22 m between opposing rear first-floor windows (houses); 3 m side separation
private_open_space: 1–2 bedroom 48 m²; 3–5 bedroom 60–75 m²; inner urban/mews 25 m²
public_open_space_pct: Greenfield 15%; brownfield/infill 10% min
car_parking_dwelling: DM Table 8(a) Limerick City and Environs (max): Dwelling <3 bed — Zone 1 0.5 / Zone 2 1 / Zone 3 1.5 spaces per unit; Dwelling 3 bed+ — Zone 1 0.75 / Zone 2 1.5 / Zone 3 2 spaces per unit; Apartment 1-2 bed — Zone 1 0.5 / Zone 2 1 / Zone 3 1 per unit; Apartment 3 bed — Zone 1 1 / Zone 2 1.5 / Zone 3 2 per unit. DM Table 8(b) Newcastle West / other settlements has separate ratios (e.g. Dwelling <3 bed 0.8–1 space per unit).
trigger_AA: European sites / protected areas screening required, especially for rural development and heritage-sensitive locations
trigger_EIA: Referenced; thresholds per Schedule 1 of P&D Regulations 2001
trigger_FRA: Flood Risk Justification Test for development in flood-prone areas (Guidelines Nov 2009)
trigger_AHIA: Design Statement for sensitive sites; archaeological clearance / curtilage of Protected Structures assessment
trigger_TTA: RIA & TIA for retail; Mobility Management Plan for significant trip-generation and enterprise >1,000 m²; School Travel Plan for schools
residential.housing_mix: A detailed breakdown of the proposed unit type and size including a percentage split between 1/2/3+ bed units including site and/or floor plans that clearly identify proposed units. This should demonstrate accordance with the Housing Need Demand Assessment.
residential.dwelling_min_floor_areas: The minimum size of habitable rooms for houses shall conform with national guidelines/standards in operation at the date of application for planning permission, including the minimum dimensions as set out in Sustainable Urban Housing: Design Standards for New Apartments Guidelines for Planning Authorities (2020) and Quality Housing for Sustainable Communities: Best Practice Guidelines for Delivering Homes Sustaining Communities (2007).
residential.apartment_min_floor_areas: All apartment developments shall accord with or exceed the minimum floor areas indicated in the Sustainable Urban Housing: Design Standards for New Apartments, Guidelines for Planning Authorities (2020). Table DM 3 Minimum Overall Apartment Floor Areas: Studio 37 m2; One bedroom 45 m2; Two bedroom (3 persons) 63 m2; Two bedroom (4 persons) 73 m2; Three bedrooms 90 m2. In order to safeguard standards, the majority of apartments in any proposed scheme of 10 or more units shall exceed the minimum floor area standard for any combination of 1, 2 or 3 bed, by a minimum of 10% (excluding studios).
residential.apartment_dual_aspect_pct: Specific Planning Policy Requirement (SPPR) 4 of the Sustainable Urban Housing: Design Standards for New Apartments, Guidelines for Planning Authorities (2020) ... In accordance with this guidance: There shall generally be a minimum of 50% dual aspect apartments in a single scheme. For building refurbishment schemes on sites of any size or urban infill schemes on sites of up to 0.25ha, Limerick City and County Council may exercise discretion to consider dual aspect unit provision at a level lower than the 50% minimum outlined above on a case-by case basis, but subject to the achievement of overall high design quality in other aspects.
residential.apartment_storage: Minimum Storage Space Requirements: One Bedroom: 3m2; Two Bedroom (3 person): 5m2; Two Bedroom (4 person): 6 sq. m.; Three Bedroom: 9m2. Storage should be additional to kitchen presses and bedroom furniture. Hot press/boiler space will not count as general storage. No individual storage room should exceed 3.5m2 and shall be provided within the apartment unit. In addition to the minimum apartment storage requirements, apartment schemes should provide external storage for bulky items outside individual units (i.e. at ground or basement level).
residential.extensions: Front extensions, at both ground and first level will be considered acceptable in principle subject to scale, design and impact on visual and residential amenities. A break in the front building line will be considered subject to scale and design and impact on established residential and visual amenity. A minimum driveway length of 6 m. should be maintained where appropriate. Ground floor rear/side extensions will be considered in terms of their length, height, proximity to mutual boundaries and quantum of usable rear private open space remaining. First floor rear/side extensions will be considered on their merits and will only be permitted where the Planning Authority is satisfied that there will be no significant negative impacts on surrounding residential or visual amenities. (11.4.4.1)
residential.dormer_standard: Roof alterations/expansions to main roof profiles (changing the hip-end roof of a semi-detached house to a gable/'A' frame end or 'half-hip' for example) and additional dormer windows will be assessed having regard to the following: The character and size of the structure, its position on the streetscape and proximity to adjacent structures; Established streetscape character and roof profiles; Dormer extensions to roofs, i.e. to the front, side and rear, will be considered with regard to impacts on existing character and form and the privacy of adjacent properties. (11.4.4.1.3)
residential.boundary_treatment_height: a) Rear boundaries for residential units shall have a minimum height of 1.8m – 2m and consist of capped, rendered concrete block/brick walls. Any wall visible from public road or open space shall be finished appropriately with suitable plaster or dash finish; b) Extensive lengths of boundary walls should not face onto the public thoroughfare; c) Provision at a minimum of concrete post and concrete panel fencing as the side boundary between the flank side walls of houses; d) Where feasible, mature tree and hedge boundaries will be incorporated into the landscaping of a scheme. (11.3.10)
residential.naming: The Council will seek that the naming of streets and residential estates shall reflect local place names or local people of note, heritage, language or topographical features as appropriate. The applicant/developer should ensure that the chosen place name for a new residential development is appropriate relative to its location and is not already in use within Limerick. (11.3.1)
residential.taking_in_charge: In residential developments, which are not proposed to be Taken in Charge by the Council, evidence will be required to show that private Management Companies will be in place by the time of completion of the estate (of which membership shall be compulsory for all purchasers of property). ... All roads, cycle lanes, footpaths, sewers, drains, lighting columns, water mains, services and open spaces within the privately managed areas ... shall be satisfactorily completed to the standard for development works as set out in the Council's 'Development Works Guidance Document'. ... the applicant shall have regard to the ... 'Taking in Charge of Residential Developments Circular Letter PD 1/08' and 'Circular Letter PL 5/2014'. (11.3.2)
residential.build_to_rent: All proposed BTR accommodation must comply with SPPR 7 and SPPR 8 as set out within the Design Standards for New Apartments, 2020 (and any amending SPPR as appropriate). ... applications for proposed BTR must clearly demonstrate compliance with the guidelines and include details in relation to: The proposed ownership and operation by an institutional entity for a minimum period of not less than 15 years and no individual residential unit can be sold or rented separately for that period. A covenant or legal agreement shall be submitted and entered into in this regard. ... All units must provide for private open space in the form of a balcony or terrace. A reduction in the area of private open space serving each unit will only be considered where at least an additional 10% high quality, useable, communal and/or additional compensatory communal support facilities are provided. ... the applicant shall clearly demonstrate that the BTR development is located within a 10 minute walking time from high frequency public transport routes. (11.4.4.9)
residential.student_accommodation: All proposals for student accommodation should comply with the Department of Education and Science Guidelines on Residential Development for Third Level Students (1999), the subsequent supplementary document (2005), the provision of the 'National Student Accommodation Strategy' (2017) and Circular PL8/2016. ... The Council will prioritise student accommodation on campus or within 1km distance from the boundary of a Third Level Institute ... the Planning Authority will consider the cumulative impact of student accommodation, which exists in the locality and will resist the over-concentration of such schemes in any one area. (11.4.4.7)
residential.bonds: To ensure the satisfactory completion of development works, such as roads, surface water drainage, public lighting and open space, including the protection of trees on a site, which has been the subject of a grant of permission, a bond or cash lodgement may be required until the development has been satisfactorily completed. The amount of such bond or cash lodgement will be determined by the Planning Authority. (11.3.3)
open_space.public_open_space_pct: In accordance with the 2009 Sustainable Residential Guidelines and any subsequent guidelines, at a minimum, 15% of the gross greenfield sites should be provided as multi-functional open space in new residential developments easily accessible to all ... In brownfield sites or infill sites, a minimum of 10% may be provided as public open space. Residential developments of 5 units or less may be exempt from the 15% open space provision on greenfield sites. (11.3.6)
open_space.public_open_space_qual: Open Space Hierarchy within Residential Estates (Table DM 1): Play lot/pocket Park 400m2 – 0.2ha, every home within 100m walking distance — all residential areas in excess of 50 units should incorporate a play lot provided at a rate of 4sqm per residential unit, developer required to provide a minimum of two permanent play low maintenance features on site; Small Park 0.2 ha – 2 ha, every residential unit within 400m walking distance, to accommodate a small playground with a minimum of 4 play features; Local Park 2 ha – 20 Ha, residences to be within 1km. Note: Ancillary open space such as grass verges, edges or corners should not be included in calculations; areas of archaeological interest shall not be included in the public open space calculation. A reduction in open space may be considered for residential units within 100m walking distance of a pocket park/play lot, small park, local park, urban neighbourhood park or regional park. Houses shall not be permitted to back onto open spaces.
open_space.private_open_space_house: The following is required as a minimum for new housing developments (excluding apartments and sheltered housing): Front garden: Minimum length of 6m. Rear Garden: Minimum 11m (22m back to back) garden depth will apply in order to protect privacy, sunlight and avoid undue overlooking. Table DM 2 Rear Garden Areas: 1-2 bedroom 48 sqm; 3-5 bedroom 60-75 sqm; Inner urban/infill dwellings/mews 25 sqm. Narrow strips of incidental open space to the side of houses should not be included in private open space calculations. (11.3.7)
open_space.balcony_min_areas: All units must provide for private open space in the form of a balcony or terrace. A reduction in the area of private open space serving each unit will only be considered where at least an additional 10% high quality, useable, communal and/or additional compensatory communal support facilities are provided. (BTR, 11.4.4.9)
open_space.play_space: All residential areas in excess of 50 units should incorporate a play lot provided at a rate of 4sqm per residential unit; Play lot should be overlooked with sufficient passive surveillance by as many houses as possible; Not permitted to side or rear of dwellings; Developer will be required to provide a minimum of two permanent play low maintenance features on site. The design shall reflect nature based play solutions. Small Park (0.2 ha – 2 ha) to accommodate a small playground with a minimum of 4 play features, kick around space and passive recreation. (Table DM 1)
community.childcare_provision: Where a new childcare facility is proposed as part of a new residential or commercial development, the facility shall be constructed in tandem with the overall scheme and shall be completed prior to residents moving in. ... the applicant shall submit a map showing locations of childcare facilities within the vicinity of the subject site and demonstrate the need for an additional facility at that location ... Properties with childcare should include a residential component within the dwelling and preferably should be occupied by the operator or a staff member of the childcare facility ... the Planning Authority will consult with the Limerick Childcare Committee to assess the need for the type of facility proposed at the intended location. (11.5.1)
community.social_infrastructure_audit: A Sustainability and Social Infrastructure Statement, which is an outline of the proposal's context and addresses how it responds to Draft Plan objectives and surroundings, should be submitted for: 10 or more dwellings; or 500m2 gross of retail, commercial/office development in urban areas; or Enterprise and employment developments over 1,000m2 gross. The statement should outline a justification for the development as proposed and any alternative design options considered. The statement should examine the impact of the proposal on social infrastructure. (11.1.2)
community.education: The Planning Authority will consider school developments having regard to specific requirements of the Department of Education and Skills (DES) and guidance set out within The Provision of Schools and the Planning System, A Code of Practice for Planning Authorities (2008). ... A School Travel Plan/Mobility Management Plan to be submitted as part of the application ...
